titleStmt.title: The Assistant Secretary of State ( (Dunn) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Assistant Secretary of State ( Dunn ) to the Secretary of State
div.p:
4333. Delsec 404 from Dunn. Meeting was held at FonOff on April 17 regarding disposition of Ital cables attended by representatives of FonOff, Post Office and Treasury, US representatives being accompanied by McIntyre of Embassy.The American representation at this meeting was headed by Jacques Reinstein, and the British group was headed by Rodney Alexander Gallop, Head of the General Department of the British Foreign Office. William H. J. McIntyre was a Treasury Attaché in the American Embassy at London. Gallop of FonOff said Brit had considered matter further in light of discussion of February 21 with RadiusWalter A. Radius, Adviser, Office of Transport and Communications Policy, assigned as an Assistant Economic Adviser to the United States Delegation to the Council of Foreign Ministers. and Reinstein (Delsec 205, Embtel 2261, February 23Not printed; it reported that Reinstein and Radius had had their first discussion with British Foreign Office and Post Office officials on the disposition of the Italian cables (740.00119 Council/2–2346).) and were now prepared to limit their claims to following cables: 1.
Malaga–Fayal cable, cut and diverted in July 1940 to Gibraltar. 2.
Rome–Malaga cable cut in two places in June 1940 and later extended to Gibraltar by adding part of Malaga–Lisbon cable. 3.
Lapanne–Lisbon cable. Operation of this cable was interrupted in Channel in 1940 prior to Italian entry into war. Italy requested Britain for help in restoring service which was refused. By operations begun in 1941 to join it to Vigo–Gibraltar cable, it was made into connection from Port Curno (Cornwall) to Gibraltar. 4.
Malaga–Lisbon cable, cut and diverted to form Caravelas (Lisbon)–Gibraltar connection. 5.
Las Palmas (Canaries)–St. Vincente (Cape Verde Islands), cut in August 1940. Part of this cable was picked up and relaid to form Gibraltar–Casablanca cable using terminal of Brest–Casablanca owned by French. This action was taken in preparation for Casablanca Conference. 6.
Malaga–Las Palmas cable cut in August 1940. Southern end was diverted (name not clear) from Las Palmas to Madeira, northern end having been diverted to Gibraltar.

Reasons given in connection with specific cables were as follows: 1.
This cable makes useful alternative route between Gibraltar and US as well as between Gibraltar and UK. 2.
Primary consideration is security. This channel was escape route for messages from Central Europe which Brit could not control. 3 and 4.
Same consideration plus fact that cables add to capacity on main through route to Far East, particularly during war. 5 and 6.
Principally security reasons.

In presenting Brit proposals, Gallop emphasized fact that action taken by Brit had been in interest of war effort and underlined security
considerations. Based position on possibility that Italy might at some time again become a pawn of other powers and necessity for securing control over these channels of communication. If they are brought under control, there would be complete control over all cables from Central Europe. He mentioned fact that Itals had interfered with Brit communication system in the Mediterranean, causing Brit great difficulty during war, as conditioning factor in Brit thinking. Ownership of overseas cable system was, he said, part of Ital effort to become great power. Italy would no longer play this role and cables should be taken over by country possessing great power responsibility and world-wide interests. He emphasized reasonableness of Brit position, pointing out Brit do not propose Italy be deprived of Mediterranean cables but merely those of strategic value.

Reinstein, representing US, said he did not feel Brit proposals would be favorably received in Washington. He said as he understood Brit proposal Italy would be stripped of her overseas cables except for Anzio–Barcelona cable and whatever parts are left of South Amer cables beyond Las Palmas. US position has been that Italy should be left with workable cable system. He outlined general US position on Ital treaty and said that argument on reparations would be greatly complicated by Brit proposals. It would be difficult to persuade Sov of seriousness or honesty of our position if they were to observe other powers busily stuffing valuable Ital assets into their pockets. US position on Amer claims against Italy had not been finally determined as yet and would no doubt be influenced to considerable measure by provisions of treaty as a whole. He hoped US-Ital settlement would be generous but felt the possibility of such a settlement would be adversely influenced if other powers insisted upon taking what they could get from Italy.

Reinstein said he could not see why it would be necessary for Brit to have ownership of Malaga-Horta cable, for example, in order to use it for communication purposes nor why Brit interest in this cable was superior to that of US. He said Department would undoubtedly wish to consider security considerations urged by British. He questioned whether matter should be approached from viewpoint urged by British, pointing out that in US viewpoint treaty should be such as to lay basis for voluntary Italian participation in UNO and admission into community of peace-loving states. He characterized British proposals as constituting reparations and questioned existence of any other legal basis for British proposal. British said they had been informed by Legal Advisor that they have sound claim and referred again to the fact that actions taken by them have been part of belligerent operations and had been taken prior to US entry into war. Reinstein said US doubted soundness of legal position, referring to Hague
Convention. Fact that British had cut and diverted cables during the war did not appear to have any significance with regard to ownership after the war. He questioned British suggestion that cables could be regarded as analogous to war booty.

At conclusion of discussion British emphasized strongly strategic considerations. They said they would not insist upon complete ownership of cables. They would be prepared to have Italian participation, if necessary to give Italians minority holding in cables. Tolls and commercial considerations were not primary factors in their position. What they are interested in is control of channels of communication.

Reinstein said he would report conversation in detail to Department but reiterated he did not feel Department would be disposed to accept British proposals. He thought that considerations against taking cables very serious and only the weightiest reasons would influence Department to change its position. He personally did not consider that reasons advanced by British were sufficient.

Gallop asked what proposal US would make. Reinstein replied that US had considered question as matter of general policy and had concluded cables should be returned to Italy. US Government had not had full details on technical adjustments which had been made in cables by British. He thought US would be reasonable considering any technical problems which might be necessitated by adoption of general policy we are urging. Obviously we would not expect British completely to relay cables themselves or to engage in other operations involving great expense. At any rate he would urge adoption this viewpoint. He did not feel that US had necessary information to: make suggestions and that any useful proposals should come from British. British asked that they be informed as soon as possible of US viewpoint. Reinstein replied that prompt answer would be sought and communicated to British at Paris.

After meeting DavidThe reference here is presumably to A. G. David of the Telecommunications Section of the General Department of the British Foreign Office. sounded Reinstein out on possibility of compromise. He hinted that it might be possible to meet US position on ownership if US could see way clear to arrangement under which British would in fact maintain control of the channels of communication.
In telegram 2597, May 24, 1946, from Rome, not printed, Chargé Key transmitted a paraphrase of Admiral Stone’s comments on the arguments advanced by the British. These comments, with which Chargé Key heartily agreed, read in part as follows:
“It is not cared by anyone who actually owns the cables and British suggestion last paragraph 404 is of course ridiculous. Who controls the terminals or relay points is the whole question that is involved. American communications companies and commercial interests will be gravely injured if either terminals or relay points are in British hands.” (811.7365/5–2446)
He pressed strategic question strongly. Reinstein replied he considered it doubtful whether any compromise would be initiated by
Washington and that if British had any ideas it would be well for them to develop them.In telegram 4336, Delsec 405, April 18, 1946, from London, not printed, Assistant Secretary Dunn observed that it had been felt necessary to report at length on the discussions with the British on the disposition of the Italian cables “in view of importance of subject and possibility which appears to exist of serious clash between US and UK interests”.
